QURRENT AFFAIRS,

Mr. Baker, one of the highly-paid auditors of the
L.G.B., and the doggy man at Kinsale, are not the
Jonly persons who are putting wattles in the way of the

" Irish Ireland steam-roller.  Brother Goulding’s rail-
way—Ordinary down to 84—has emitted a shrill
whistle by way of announcing that it is going to put a

~ pebble on the Irish Ireland track. We specially direct
~ the eyes of our readers to the permanent way between

Tuam and Ballinrobe.

~ The Great Sourface Railway at Tuam is anxious to
* make a fool of itself, and the local branch of the Gaelic
League is ready and willing to help it in its charaoter-
ic enterprise. It appears that the Treasurer of the
am Branch, Mr. M. E. Walsh, is a prominent mer-

chant who has been in the habit of forwarding by rail
parcels addressed in Irish to the National School tea-
cher at Ballinrobe. Up to a few days ago the Greab
Sourface Railway people accepted and delivered these
““illegibly ”” addressed parcels. Then a change came,
and a parcels clerk of this railway, whose receipts have
toppled, and whose Ordinary Shares have slumped to
84—the parcels clerk objected. Mr. Walsh, however,
sent the parcel as usual. His Majesty, England’s
Faithful Great Sourface parcel’s clerk at Tuam, re-
fused to accept it for delivery unless the address was
written in English. Needless to say, Mr. Walsh re-
fused to be dictated to by this Great Sourface poten-
tate, and subsequently received a note from the Great
Sowrface stationmaster at Tuam stating—“I cannot
accept parcel for Ballinrobe as consignee’s name is not

legible.  Please send me label before train leaves.”’
Well, if the train waits until Mr. Walsh obeys the
dictation of this Tuam stationmaster, it will never
start.

Mr. Walsh brought the matter before the Committee
of the local branch of the Gaelic League, and the secre-
tary was directed to demand an explanation. In the
event of the Tuam Branch not getting a satisfactory
answer, that is, unless England’s Faithful boycott is
taken off Mr. Walsh’s Irish parcels, a meeting of the
traders of Tuam will be held to consider the matter.
Two can play at boycott; and now that the traffic re-
ceipts of the Great Sourface Railway are on the topple,
and the Ordinary Shares slumped to 84, the share-
holders of the Great Sourface Railway will not welcome
a conflict in such a seusitive place as Tuam.

The proceedings at the inaugural meeting of the Uni-
versity College Literary and Historical Society, whilst
Sir Christopher Nixon was speaking on Mr. T. F.
Bacon’s paper, were rather lively. The Chairman, Rev.
Dr. Delaney, very severely rebuked those who made all
the noise, and as Chairman he could have done no less.
Speakers who accept an invitation to address a meeting
are entitled to a fair hearing, though it is doubtful if
they-ever get an uninterrupted one at any meeting com-
posed partly of undergraduates in any part of the
world, even in that part of England, the site of the
Souper’s University in College Green; and as Mr.
Healy said, “Boys will be boys.”” The interrupters
wanted an answer to what after all is a quite fair and
Jegitimate question to a Catholic Irishman on a public
platform from which University matters were being dis-
cussed. The question was, “ Why did you send your
son to Trinity?’ That is not only a fair question, but
one ‘that we think should be asked. Sir Christopher
did not answer it. Men who had refused to go to
Trinity were on the platform; many boys who would
not accept the bribes of the Souper’s University were
probably at the back of the hall, and Sir Christopher
Nixon, a ‘“tame’’ Catholic, who had been to the
Souper’s University himself, and who had sent his son
there, was holding forth on the University grievance.
A. pretty picture. Surely the lads at the back of the
hall would be as “ tame '’ as Sir Christy himself if they
rofrained from asking “ Why did you send your son to
Trinity ¥’

Catholics who go to Trinity in face of the present
situation are blacklegs, and blacklegs cannot expect an
over abundance of courtesy. When Dr. McDonald of
Maynooth, proposed that the Catholics should go as
a body into Trinity, his was, and is, a voice crying in
the wilderness, When Nutting founded his bribes, the

- years. General statements are not so tellin,

B

Catholic youths of the country were very properly
warned against them. But yet a man who has gone o
the Souper’s University and who has sent his son there,
is one of the principal speakers at a meeting concerned
with the Irish Catholic University grievance. It bor-
ders on opera bouffe. Sir Christopher is to be free to
play the blackleg by sending his son to Trinity, and
no doubt he felt very pained at being asked the reason
why! We can understand and sympathise with
Dr. Delaney’s anxiety that an invited speaker
should be treated with the utmost courtesy. At
the same time it was well and wholesome that Sir
Christy was heckled. If Catholics do wrong to the
nation, to the interests of the great body to which they
belong by ratting and sneaking into Trinity, then these
-men should be punished for their backsliding. So long
as Catholics are exhorted not to go to Trinity, but are
honoured and respected when they sneak in, so long
the situation is charged with a good deal of humbug.
Sir Christopher, in the course of an eloquent passage,
was saying “ No more disgraceful ’ when a voice
added “ than sending your son to Trinity.”’ In another
part of his speech he declared “ I must confess: ” and
a “voice’’ added “that I sent my son to Trinity.”
As boys will be boys, and as undergraduates in all Uni-
versities feel that they have a noisy tradition to live up
to on occasions of this kind, we do not see that Sir
Christy has anything to complain of on the head of
these remarks. = Sir Christy lost his temper in the end.
Well, he has largely himself to blame; why did he
send his son to Trinity. If some people are not
punished for wrongdoing they will keep doing wrong.
We hope that the reception accorded to Sir Christy will
be an example and a warning to others of the “tame *’
varicty who may cast hungry eyes on the back door of
the Souper’s University.

Dr." Delaney made some pertinent remarks concera-
ing the exaggerated and rather foolish taunts levelled
at the “Royal’ on the ground that it is only an
examining body. He said:— As a matter of fact all
who had gone out of that College had been directly and
immediately taught by the Royal University. Trinity
College and the University of Dublin had no such con-
nection. The University of Dublin had not a single
penny to give prizes; it did not teach—Trinity College
taught, but not the University. He did not know in
the world a greater sham than the University of Dublin
as separate from Trinity College. There was nothing
similar to it elsewhere. On the other hand in the
Royal TUniversity there was a more direct connection
with the teaching of this College and the training of
its pupils than there was in any College of Cambridge
or Oxford or in the United Kingdom. Therefore, as
far as this College was concerned, the Royal University
was a teaching University in the fullest sense of the
word.” Dr. Delaney raked Trinity severely and de-
clared that as for its prestige hedenied it and despised
it. So after all Sir Christopher might have been wiser
from every point of view if he had sent his son to
University College. Dr. Delaney said:—“In Trinity
College they had one type of educational culture. The
students went into the schools that fed Trinity College ;
they went then into Trinity College, and ended in
Trinity College. The body that controlled the teach-
ing in the Royal University, on the other hand, re-
prosented the three Queen’s Colleges and that Univer-
sity College, and included many of the best men from

- Gormany and abroad. Therefore, they had a wider

culture applied to the teaching and examinations and
work of the Royal University. And when people spoke
to him of the prestige of Trinity College, he denjed it,

he despised. it. (Appl

As Treland is not now, except to a very small extent,
a wheat-growing country, the expansion of the flour-
milling industry would naturally synchronise with an
oxpansion of wheat imports and a contraction of flour
imports. At the Dublin port we see there was an in-
crease of 39,655 tons of wheat, and a diminution of
3,861 tons of the manufactured article—flour. The
figures for Belfast are even more remarkable, for they
show that whilst the imports of flour have fallen con-
siderably more than half, the imports of wheat have
nearly doubled. Cork port shows a fall of over 50 per
cent. in flour imports, and a very large increase in
wheat, and at Waterford also the same general ten-
dency of increase in wheat and decrease in flour is
marked. < The importance to the country of these move-
ments in wheat and flour will be the better appreciated
when we point out that according to reliable informa-
tion received every 125 tons means the expenditure of
£95 to £30 in direct wages when flour is manufactured
at home.

Now that there is so much talking and exhorting
everywhere about supporting Irish industries, a little
fact will be of interest. A practical rather than a
talking Irish Irelander, residing temporarily in Eng-
land, was at Killarney during the Munster Feis. Being
in want of a cap and seeing a big green advertisement
from one of Killarney’s shops on the Feis programme,
he went there and asked for a cap, “but even though
it was Feis week and they had an advertisement in the
Feis programme, box after box of caps—English made
—were brought for me to choose from. I refused them
all without mentioning that I wanted Irish manufac-
ture, just to see what chance they gave the Irish
article, but I might be there until now before an Irish
cap would be shown me, eventually T asked for an Irish
cap, and a box was fished up from another portion of
the shop containing a few caps of Irish make—I bought
one. Is that how Irish manufacture was treated in all
shops in Killarney during Feis Week?’

The Protestant Bishop of Killaloe, Dr. Archdale,
delivered a speech at a recent meeting of the County
Tipperary Protestant Orphan Society at Nenagh. The
Intermediate Results, the splendid work:done, and being
done, by the Christian Brothers, and the various con-
vents that devote themselves to teaching, and the extra-
ordinary work of University College, Dublin, with
which real live, undisguised Jesuits have to do, have all
combined to give the Protestant Bishop of Killaloe not
only a big, but a sort of lasting fright. Well, we don’t
wonder. He said :— With regard to the attitude of
their own clergy (the highly-paid Protestant. clergy),
they were absolutely dead as regards education '’ (hear

hear). That is a very emphatic assertion, and reflects .

very. little credit on: the highly-paid Protestant clergy,
most of whom have such small congregations: that they

DECEMBER 2, 1905.

must have a lot of time on their hands. What are they
doing to justify their existence by way of exchange for
the huge economic drain that their up-keep in this poor
country represents if they are “absolutely dead as re-
gards education?’ No wonder the Intermediate and
Royal University results reflect no great credit on the
Planters in this country..

Still harping on education, Dr. Archdale moaned :—
“The Roman Catholic Church had got a tremendous
power over them in that respect. It was making those
monastic institutions to be most successful teachers of
primary education and secondary education. This was
very easily done by multiplying teachers without any
real cost, as they were living in communities. They were
giving cheap education, yes, a free education to Protes-
tant children in order to fit them for those offices in life
which they desired to fill. If the Protestant farmer, or
the Protestant man of business, or the Protestant
labourer wanted to have his son educated for any of those
positions which people of the middle class desired for
their children, they had nothing in the world to do but
send them to the Christian Brothers’ school, and if they
| wanted to fit their girls for a position in life they sent

them to a convent school. What had they (Protestants)
{ to offer them? All through the country they had little
schools with one teacher to teach everything, and it
could be seen at once how they were an utter failure.”

Verily, this discloses quite a dreadful state of affairs.
Why should the Christian Brothers and the convents do
the work for Protestant children that Protestants—
with all their confiscations, endowments, Erasmus
Smith Schools, the Soupers’ University, etc.—have
failed to do for their own? Having been—and being
—robbed, are we to educate the Planters’ children?

The Protestant Rev. Chancellor Hackett was not ap-
parently in an over-optimistic humour either. The
ignorance of the Planters of this country is beginning
to tell on their spirits. “It was once their boast.”
said this Protestant minister, “that they were well
educated, but now he believed it was coming round the
other way—they were lagging behind in that educa-
tional race they were to run.”” Indeed, the truth of
this Protestant minister’s statement is patent to all.
The convents, the Christian Brothers, and institutions
like Fermoy College—where Irish is in esteem—and
University College, Dublin, have knocked the gilt off
the Protestant educational gingerbread.

A Mr. Toler R. Garvey—whoever he is, but we judge
him to be a man with more emphasis on the “ Toler *’
than on the “ Garvey ’’ part of him—took up the dole-
ful moan. Quoth he:—“ He was very glad education
had been the key-note of the speeches delivered there
that day, because, as his lordship truly said, a great
many of them had been too long dead to this great
question. His lordship had called their attention to it
at the Diocesan Council, and in various other places
where he had spoken, and he was in a position to know
more about the subject than any other Protestant
divine in this country. They were now in a crisis with
regard to this matter of education, but he (Mr. Garvey)
trusted they would awake, and that the Diocesan
Board of Education would take into immediate con-
sideration what could be done for the benefit of their
Protestant children throughout the diocese.””  Pity
| the plight of the poor, ignorant Planter! What doth

it profit a class to grab all if they are remiss in the cul-
} tivation of their own minds?} The Planters have got the

privilege, and the natives have got the brains. Under
i the circumstances we beg to .subscribe ourselves' as

optimists. ;

In the circles of England’s Faithful Post: Office in
Treland the cry is “still they come!’” It will be re-
membered that a Sandy, one D. MacPhail, Postmaster.
of Laurencekirk, was recently appointed Postmaster of
Roscommon. It now transpires that' Sandy wouldn’t
take the Roscommon job, and one A. E. Birkett, sort-

ing clerk and telegraphist at Bastbourne, England, has
been dumped into it in his stead. ~What does Ros-
common—and what do the unfortunate postal officials
in Ireland—think of this latest dump?

The plea of his Grace the Archbishop of Dublin for
an Irish Language Training College for Dublin is most
opportune. Pending tthe establishment of this College in
Dublin, existing Educational institutions in the city and
country could do something. Now that his Grace’s letter
is before them, perhaps the authorities of Blackrock,
and even of Castleknock, might stir themselves. Clon-
gowes might make an effort to emulate Fermoy College
in its concern for Irish., The great record of St. Col-
man’s College, Fermoy, has demonstrated what, even
under existing arbitrary conditions, a College may do
for Irish and for Ireland when its heart is in the right
place 3

It is a way with some of our new intellectuals of
“superior ’’ education to sneer at Manchester.  The
Victoria University at that abandoned centre of mere
trade, where they live by selling cotton rather than by
attempting to exist on one another’s  superior ’’ meta-
physics—the Victoria University of that mere trading
centre has established evening classes in the Irish lan-
guage. Mr. Joseph O’Neill, M.A., who conducts the
class, said:—“It is very peculiar that educationak
Manchester should be doing for the cause what nobody
in Ireland has yet been prevailed upon to do. At
Trinity College are the manuscripts, but there is no one
to use them. I have, at great labour, copied many of
these for the purpose of bringing them to Manchester.””
A Manchester contemporary, referring to these classes,
stated :—“ It is the first evening class under the xgis of
a recognised educational institution in the world, and
there were general expressions of appreciation at the con-
cessions obtained. In fact, it would be no exaggeration
to say that ‘the base, brutal, and bloody Sassenach,’ ‘the
two-faced Sassenach,” has rather bewildered Man-
chester Irishmen by raising the Irish language to a dig-
nity it has not attained in any other country.”
Promptly Mr. J. H. Reynolds, Director of Higher Edu-
cation, wrote to the Manchester Evening News, in which
he controverts the statement that the Manchester Uni-
versity was the first to start evening classes in Irish.
“Permit me to say,”’ he writes, “that the Education
Committee have now evening classes under their direc-
tion in Irish at St. Francis’s, Gorton; St. Wilfrid’s,
Hulme, and St. Patrick’s, Livesey Street. These classes
in Irish have been in operation under the late School
Board and the Education Committee during the last
four years, and last year at St. Francis’s, Gorton, and
St. Wilfrid’s, Hulme, there were 102 students.”’

We have' before us a leaflet issued by Mr. Thomas
Beaston, Head Master of St. Wilfrid’s Evening Con-

worthy of being set out in its entirety for the edification

here as to which will capture .the students studying
Trish. The Manchester Education Committee have:in-

approached the Gaelic League with a view to starting

whilst this week it is announced that the Salford Edu-

being started in one of their schools. The University

Our note in the LEADER, of November 18th, in reply
to a query from Australia, has been challenged by two
correspondents. In the course of that note we said:—

““The battle of the Irish nation must be fought out
on the soil of Ireland ; and these people who have left it
for Australia, America, or elsewhere arc practically
children of the country of their adoption, whatever that
country may be, and have ceased to be Irish. They may
come back to this country, and resume their part as
Irishmen ; and we sincerely wish that a great many per-
sons of Irish extraction or Irish birth now living as
Americans or Australians would return to Ireland with
their skill and capital, and help those on the soil to
work out the problem of Irish nation-making.”’

One correspondent asks, ‘‘ Have the hundreds of
thousands of Irishmen and women who have gone to
Australia and America ceased to be Irish and become
renegades to their country?’ We said nothing about
renegades; so that point is irrelevant. ~We might
answer this question by asking another—If Irish people
who settle abroad continue to be Irish, why all the out-
ery about, emigration ?

Our correspondent asks, ‘“ Were those Australians
dead to Irish sympathy who welcomed Dillon, Deasy,
and Esmonde some seventeen years ago and sent in
cheques as thick as snow flakes on the stage where the
delegates stood ? ’  We do not suppose they were dead
to Irish sympathy. We did not raise that question.
All we said was that such people were Australians by
adoption. An Irishman in being is one who lives his
working life in Ireland and rears his family in Ireland.
.If we can all be Irish and live and bring up our
families in Australia and America, well then let us
repeat that objecting to emigration is all nonsense
from an Irish point of view. ‘‘The other day,”” says
our correspondent, “ 6,000 Irish and Irish-Australians
attended William Redmond’s meeting in Sydney, pre-
sided over by Cardinal Moran, and sent a handsome
donation to the cause.”” Again, let us point out that
if all these 6,000 are Irish, why weep or wail over their
emigration? What country are they making, enrich-
ing by their labours and enterprise, for what country
are they rearing their families?  Our correspondent
also says: ‘‘ The Commonwealth Parliament, of Austra-
lia has lately passed a resolution favouring Iome Rule
for Ireland.”” Well, what of that? When people on
this side were passing unanimous resolutions about the
Boers, did they all become Transvaal farmers? We
cannot understand how our correspondent took excep-
tion to our note. No one has more appreciation ‘than
we of the work which a large number, though

a very small proportion, of Irish exiles do to
help Ireland within the scope of their limited oppor-
tunities, and we feel convinced that no people are more
keenly. conscious of the truth of our general proposition
that those' who have settled in other countries belong to

‘these. countries than the handful of earnest workers

who have done so much according to their opportunities
in ‘organising the recent London dona¢. Were the con-
verse to our proposition true, emigration would cease
to be an Irish National evil:

THE LEADER.

DECEMPER 2, 1905,

The Medical Profession in Ireland are going to have
their General, Election. A representative on the
General Medical Council will be selected early in. the
next year. This Body is a mere Board of Directors
elected mainly by the-doctor-making Bodies whose in-
torests, needless to -say, they exclusively guard. It
thus comes to pass that the flocks are fleeced while the
shepherds grow fat! Eyer since the whole. Irish pro-
fession' got a representative at all they, like the Roman
Plebs before them, returned one of the classes. First
it was a Dr. Kidd, and then Sir W, Thompson. Need-
less to say the masses who ignored themselves aro mer-
cilossly lashed by their task masters. Dr. Laffan, who
avers that while no Protestant voted for him, the bulk
of the provincial Papists in their desperate anxiety to
bend the knee to their shoneen superiors followed
humbly suite. It would seem also that the strictness of
the medical cxaminations is under grave suspicion, that
the classes monopolise all the teaching, and that through
the instrumentality of their Hospitals and Homes they
aro fast appropriating all the well-to-do patients-and
leaving nothing to the provincials but the workhouse.
Well, we shall see if the plundered ones will go on tak-
ing their beating lying down, or if they will rise up
against their oppressors and return one of themselves.

Mr. Eamonn O’Neill, of Kinsale, who recently served
a term of imprisonment over his attempt Lo license his
historic dog, sent the following letter to the presiding
magistrate at the last fortnightly meeting of the local
Petty Sessions:—

Sir,—In accordance with the order made by the Bench at last
Potty Sossions, I applied yesterday to Mr. Forde for a licence
for my dog. He refused to entertain it, however, though it was
written in clear legible Roman characters. I can find no law
to justify his action, and he did not mention his authority for re-
jecting my application. This scems a very arbitrary action on his
part, and I beg that you will be good enough to give him instruc-
tions to issue the licence to me in the manner I desiro. I have
lately, since the iniquitous proceedings at last Sessions, been ad-
vised that thero is no legal ground whatever for the refusal. In
theso circumstances, then, I request that you will look into the
matter, and instruct the C.P.S. to carry out the order made this
day fortnight. If you have any reason to believe that there is
any clause in the Dogs Act to justify his or your attitudo towards
Irish, kindly point it out to me, so that I may not again he
branded as a “criminal” for daring to make use of my native
language. Requesting the favour of a reply.—Yours, oto.,

EAMONN O’NEILL, B.A.

England’s Faithful Guardians on the Kinsale Petty
Bench read the letter, but took no action. It was a
wonder they even condescended to read it .

While one Hogg, a constable, is looking after the cart
of Témsp Mac Soin in the place in Roscommon Eng-
lished as Strokestown, one Bagge, a constable, is looking
after an Irish Irelander’s dog in Kinsale. One thinks
of the broad comic relief of some of Shakespeare’s
comedies when he contemplates the labours of Hogg and
Bagge, constables. Seasmup Dpeatnac and his dumb
maojfia are under the surveillance of Bagge, a constable
of old Kinsale.  Seamup Ujpeatna¢, according to
Bagge’s story, has an unlicensed maopa in his posses-

sion; Bagge, a constable, needless to say, calls it a -

d-o-g, dog. Unlike Hogg, a constable, Bagge, a con-
stable, knows Irish. One Forde—even the name sug-
gests Shakespeare’s broad comedy—a . clerk to Petty

Sessions, deposed that several persons applied to him"

for licenses in Irish, which he, Forde, refused to give.
Seamur asked for the license in Irish, and Forde re-
fused. Mr. O’Sullivan, solicitor, who represeunted
Seamup Upeatna¢ produced an official Inland notice,
printed on one side in English, and on the other side in
Woelsh, and a notice as to dog licences, in Welsh, simi-
larly printed, and handed them to the Bonch. Even-
tually Seamup peatna¢ was fined 6d. and costs, to
take out a license; or in default to be imprisoned for
one day. The trusty and rusty-looking terriers are
doing their share for the language movement. In our
Christmas Number next week we will deal with this

THE INDUSTRIAL CONFERENCE.

R. WINDLE, in his opening remarks at the In-

dustrial Conference, stated that in 1904, pairs
of boots to the number of 82,549 were imported into
Ireland from Boston alone, their value being £27,446.
Now it so happens that a boot manufacturer in Boston
made an offer to start the boot industry in Ireland, and
there was much negotiating about the manner. An at-
tempt was made to get Waterford to take the matter up
but it came to nothing, or at least so far it has come to
nothing. It was part of the scheme that a certain
amount of capital should be raised in Ireland. The
Boston manufacturer being a business man, made his
calculations subject to strict market value, and did not
expect either private or public grants in aid; but the
capital was not forthcoming. Perhaps Cork would give
the Boston manufacturer’s scheme a better welcome
than Waterford did. If Cork would like to enquire
into it we are sure that Father Skelly, O.P., now in
Tallaght, Co. Dublin, would give them all the informa-
tion in his power, and that would be a considerable
amount, for he made great efforts to help the scheme to
fruition.

The Most Rev. Dr. Browne in proposing that tech-
nical education should be so ordered as to fit in with the
needs of existing industries and those that might pro-
fitably be started, said:—If the country is really in
earnest in its determination to develop native manu-
factures and trades, we must be prepared to face com-
petition from England, Germany, the United States,
and all the countries where similar-articles are made
and whence they are imported to the cities and towns
and villages of Ireland and dumped by: travellers and
hawkors on the: doorsteps of our country hamlets, Thexe
is no use in our facing this compebition unless we are in
a position to put on the market as good and as cheap an

“article.”” During the same discussion Father. Dowling,

C.M., suggested that there should be a fund of £50,000
or'£100,000‘ in connection with the Department, that
this money should: be: used' here: and there for the pur-
pose of starting new industries which when placed on
a paying basis could be sold, and the money realised
re-used for a similar purpose. Was that not a practi-
cal suggestion, asked Father Dowling. We fear it is

- DECEMBER 2, 1905.

= little too practical for the Department. Any objec-

tion to it on the ground of its interfering with private
-enterprise, would not hold good in the case. of new in-

. dustuies, and there are plenty of industries non-cxistent

in Treland at present that could be started with advan-
tage to the country.

_ The Bishop of Waterford made an excellent speech
in proposing the resolution, urging upon public Boards
to give Irish goods a decided proference. It may be
remembered that originally the resolution contained the
words ““ decided proference for the next five years .

oven though at the moment, some of them may be
sll_gh(;ly higher in price.”” We wrote an article upon
Lh}s resolution in our issue of September 16th, and
- Subsequently we noticed that the resolution was altered.
‘We have so often and so oxhaustively dealt with the
non-calculating, though no doubt warm-hearted and
well-meaning sentimentalists who think that they do a
service instead of a serious injury to Irish industrial
interests by dressing Irish industry up as a sort of
mendicant asking for alms, that we will not dwell at any
length on this question now. Having had occasion to
defend the general position off and on ever since we
started, and finding that there were still unbelievers, we
did what for all practical purposes put an end to the
<ontroversy. We put the issue straight to manufac-
turers all over the country and asked them for their
verdict. And the unanimous verdict of every manu-
facturer who replied is on record in our files and that
settled the matter for all practical people. The Bishop
of Waterford in proposing the resolution as amended,
was careful to explain, and he was nothing if not pre-
«cise about it, how he interpreted what he was proposing.
He said : — Therefore they should endeavour to induce
the public Boards to give a preference, in the words of
‘the resolution, a decided preference, to articles of Irish
manufacture. He, for his part, did not mean by de-
cided preference that they should be called upon to pay
more for an Irish article than for a foreign, or to take
an inferior article, because it was Irish, at the same
price. What he meant was that the articles being
equal in price and quality, they should give the pre-
ference to the Irish article.”

Captain Shawe-Taylor, who is no doubt a careful stu-
dent of the LEADER, summarised with ability
‘many of the points that we have urged from time to
time on this question. Captain Shawe-Taylor re-
marked—*“ And so while they did not ask for a finan-
cial preference, they did most earnestly appeal to all the
public Boards of this country, North and South, to
give a decided preference to Irish-made goods.”” And
Mr. Irwin, T.C., of Dublin, a well-known paper manu-
facturer himself, said :— There could be no more dan-
gerous or prejudicial notion than that Irish industries
were to be fostered and built up by penalising the
public.”” The resolution proposed so ably by the Most
Rev. Dr. Sheehan, was passed unanimously.

Of course general principles are general principles,
and people who interpret them are supposed to have

. some gumption. Exceptions can be treated om their

merits. There are some what we may call illogically
Togical people who would magnify an exception so much
that the general principle to which it was an exception
would appear so relatively small that the exception
‘would be exalted into the place of the general principle.

‘We hope that practical results will follow from the
question of a National Trade Mark that was discussed
at the Conference. Interesting discussions took place
on the transit question—one of the most vital elements
in the complex industrial problem—and on the ever-
green—if we may wuse the word in this connection—
‘topic of reafforestation. After all the interesting talk,
and the unanimous resolutions it will be a queer thing
if—only to take one item—the consumers of Cork are
mot able to clear, or nearly clear, the tobacco Combine
-with all their foreign goods and chattels, not forgetting
‘the free, gratis, and for-nothing window dressers out of
‘the city.

By way of a few general comments we might point

‘out that the LEADER policy, or as it is sometimes called,

ithe Trish Ireland or Sinn Femn policy, is in its essence

THE 'LEADER:." : L og]

an attempt to turn men’s minds to wha.t can be, done in
the living present under existing conditions and exist-
ing nabional disabilities. ~We seldom or ever trouble
our readers with what could be done if we had Home
Rule, and this, that, and the other which, as a matter
of fact, we do not possess. We will talk of
buying up the railways and many other things when
we have a Home Government. There are a great many
things that should be done, and ought to be done, but
can’t be done-until we have the arrangement of our
own affairs; and it is little use talking of what we
should or should not do if we were in a position other
than we are. These questions may be left on the shelf
—oxcept when they arc temporarily taken down for
academic discussion or merely educational purposes—
until we have the power to deal with them. There are
a number of people—be they more or less—who believe
that Ireland should withdraw her representatives from
the floor of the British House of Commons. We who
would work our policy of Sinn e for all it is worth.
would also work the floor of .the British House of Com-
mons so long as anything could be got out of it. At
the present juncture when a General Election and a re-
arrangement of parties in England are at hand, it is
highly probable that a fairly efficient Parliamentary
Party may be able to do a considerable amount of
effective work. In the next British Parliament the
members from Ireland should be able to do much for
the interests of Irish industry, and tackle effectively,
to some extent, some of the problems that engaged the
attention of the Industrial Conference at Cork, if the
Press that concerns itself with industrial matters,
public opinion and the various Industrial Associations,
keep the members up to their work.

THE COMING EVENT.

N amiable noodle in one of Dickens’s books pro-
nounced Lord Byron to be “a very clever man,’
an encomiastic dictum, expressed in words unsuitable
enough to be droll. Lord Frederick Verisopht’s opinion
of Byron might be applied to Mr. Joseph Chamberlain
without any reserves. He 4s a very clever man, and an
able hand at forcing a situation. The latest situation
he has set himself to force is one in which we have an
interest, and should the long-delaying Balfour at last
clothe himself with the melancholious virtue of resigna-
tion, it were time we Irish should make up our minds

which way we should try to make the cat jump.

A few cold reflections may be wholesome.  There
seems to be no serious doubt about the accession of the
Liberal Party to power. For some reason we associate
the Liberals with “hope for Ireland,”” and the Tories
with the other thing. Upon the reasonableness of this
habit of fancy I say nothing. Let kopes be what they
will, certainties are certainties, and one permanent cer-
tainty is this: that in respect of anything they care to
block, the Tories can always counter off the Liberals
with the House of Lords. In the end, the situation
commonly comes to this: that the Liberals have sym-
pathy, and the Tories power. Even at the lowest ebb
of their fortunes, the Tories have always the power to
nullify the Liberal sympathy. In the final event of
all, it is pressure in Ireland that makes Ministries move.

When the Liberals were last in office, (tis ten years
sincel) what happened? Being defeated on their
greatest measure, they clung on to office, refusing to go
to the country on the very subject which brought ‘nto
oxigtence the new party name of “Unionist.” They
could not have stayed half-a-day in office, had not the
Irish members, on whom their ministerial existence de-
pended, kopt them there. In return for this, for the
sheer possibility of existence, what did the anti-Parnel-
lites get? Nothing that I know off—except “ Davitt *’
magistracies. The only other political memory of that
time which remains with me is the phrase, “ Don’t em-
barrass the Government,”” " The Iadependent, then the

live organ of a lively little Party, worked the phrase
 (sarcastically) for all it was worth. The phrase was
useful to the Parnellites, who came back from the elec-
tions of 95 with an increased following; the policy
“which the phrase represented was useless to those who
pursued it—the “official”’ Nationalists, or, as they
will be known to history, the anti-Parnellites.
. The moral of all this is clear: Give nothing for noth-
ang! Do not keep a Government in power without a
clear, definite bargain as to what you are to get from
it. I say bargain; no mere understanding ** will do.
Once is quite enough to be “ codded *’ that way. Not,
indeed, that the anti-Parnellites had, so far as I know,
even an “ understanding *’ with the Liberals as to the
terms on which they would keep the latter in power;
they don’t seem to have had cuteness enough to arrive
even at that. Their Chairman, Mr. Justin McCarthy,
assured a wondering world that ho had the Liberal
Government “in the hollow of his hand.”” And so he
had—but it was not the hand of the man whom Mr.
Morley likened to Cavour, so nothing came of the
situation. Now, whatever else occurs again, this must
not. The Irish Party is largely paid for by the Irish
people, and I can conceive no more lunatic situation
than the keeping of an English Party in office gratis,
at the expense of a people whose money is little enough
for themselves. I have said that no mere “under-
standing ”’ will do, and it will not. Cardinal New-
man, in the Apologia, says that a very prudent man
once warned him to have nothing to do with “ under-
standings.””  The great Tractarian mentions this in
connection with a taking-in he had experienced over an
“ understanding ’’ himself. The great Powers never do
anything momentous on the strength of a mere entente;
they take care to have a treaty—a hard, clear, written
bargain. .Let our people have the like. It need not
be published to the world, but it should have a definite,
real existence, in black and white, whether as a letter,
or in whatever other tangible form the circumstances
make convenient. But a bargain there should be, and
no loose ““ understandings,”” that bind nobody, and in-
volve nothing. Votes are definite things, and eighty
of them should not be given in return for nothing. As
for any question of gratitude to the Liberals, that does
not arise. - Their not dissolving on the Home Rule
question in ’93, their clinging on to office, and inglorious
going out of it (over cordite!), together with their pro-
longed silence about Home Rule ever since—all these
things have wiped away all weak considerations of
“gratitude.””. It should be made as clear as not merely
words, but actions, if necessary, can make it, that we

HEN one looked on the stage and saw and
“ heard the splendid production of “Gisippus”
by Tearle’s Company at the Queen’s Theatre, one
wished that the dead figure from the North Monastery
at Cork were privileged to visit this sphere and be
present at the play. But when one glanced
around the theatre and saw the beggarly array of
empty boxes, the tiers of empty seats, one felt that all
things happen for the best, and that it was well that
the gentle spirit of Griffin was spared the anguish of
seeing the audience which his tragedy attracted even at
the full flood of the Gaelic Revival when, as we were
told, there was an eager longing to give an ardent wel-
come to anything that was tolerable, not to say sublime,
from the pen of an Irish author. We have seen these
benches thronged with an eager crowd who made the
welkin ring with applause when the play was of the
“ Kitty-Grey *’ type, or when something was shown after
the style of “Sappho.” To see “ Gisippus’’ as it ap-
peared this week was a liberal education, every line
was so faultlessly delivered as to be a lesson in elocution,
and the stage settings and costumes were so accurate
as to be a lesson in Roman History. A whole term
spent in cramming a classic text at a school could not
impart so much culture as an afternoon’s attendance
at the tragedy, yet even the schools, which were in-
vited to come at half-price were absent.

Lveryone knows that the plot is of the slenderest kind.
It turns on the friendship of Gisippus who gives up
more than his life—Sophronia, the fairest maid in
Athens—for his friend Fulvius. The creditors of Gisippus
are so enraged at the loss of the lady’s fortune that
they seize him and sell him for a slave, whilst Fulvius
is recalled to Rome by the Emperor and given a com-
mand. He is victorious in his arms, and returning, is
granted an ovation by the Senate, whilst Gisippus,
abject and despised amongst men, is shunned by his
soldiery as'a Greek beggar, and becomes so weary of
life that when he is falsely charged with murder he
welcomes the block. When the executioner’s axe is about
to descend, Fulvius, now Praetor, appears, and kneeling,
begs his friend’s pardon for his neglect. Gisippus spurns
him at {irsb, and insists on the boon of death. Then
Sophronia appears and for her sake he becomes recon-
ciled, and accepts a command in the Roman army.

Some of Griffin’s admirers have compared him witlh:
Shakespeare, and this much is certain, that if passages
like this from “ Gisippus’’ were given at the Interme-
diate, and the name of the author required, 90 pev
cent. of the answers would state the “ Bard of Avon.’”

“ The mystery of Fulvius—and

Sophronia’s old affection? You great gods,

I see my fate—The sacrifice you ask

Is great and bitter—You may lay upon me
This heavy test—lift up my soul to meot

And wrestle with its potency—the hour

Is come at length when the young votary, Virtue,
Must prove his' worship real-—when the spirit
Shall soar above all natural affections

A wonder and a tale for days unbor’n

Or sink degraded, into self.’” :

EDUCATION XS K HELP, AND A8 K HUMBUG.

0 HE government of Ireland is conducted somewhat
on the principle of one.of Lever’s novels, and
so far that writer cannot be accused of untruthfulness
to the facts of Irish life. The portrait of the Irish-
man always spending lavishly and yet never having the
money for anything, finds a sufficiently close original
in the managemont of Dublin Castle. Apart from Im-
perial tribute, our government costs us about eight
millions ; whilst the cost of any other decently managed
country of similar proportions, is only four. And yet
when you seek money for any purpose from the draining
of the Bann to the payment of National teachers, it is
not to be had. In one or two departments indeed,
such as the law and the police, we see the waste going
on under our eyes, but in most matters public money
flows away like the Poddle in an underground course
and no one is the better of it. .
* We hear pretty often of developing Irish resources,
and nowadays Chief Secretaries indulge in motor-rides
in search of them. All sorts of wild schemes are out-
lined. Minerals are discovered in the most impossible
places and the transmutation of turf is talked about
much as if that substance were the philosopher’s stone.
Unusual methods of transit are proposed, and great
projects hatched. The Government builds an occa-
sional pier or provides a bull or a hen to give an air
of reality to the proceedings. But really is it not often
best to do things in a trite way? I should be quite con-
tented if the Government and their pet philanthropists
gave up their endeavour to develop those marvellous
Ivish resources, which no one is able to get at, if they
decided in fact to give up their exceptional efforts to
help us and confined themselves to doing merely or-
dinary things, educating the people for instance, which
less benevolent Governments do in other countries.
How little the one resource we really have, the resource
of intellect, is attempted to be developed in Ireland was
Mr. Thomas Bacon’s theme at the recent University
College Inaugural. It is rather a pity that he was sub-
jected to a good deal of interruption, for his paper was
an extremely well-written one. The interruption of
thie following speaker had a meaning; but “ motiveless
disorder ’—to quote from a recent article—is not a
commodity to be encouraged. In Dublin University na
doubt it is often quite impossible to hear the auditor at
all, yet let us hope Catholic University men will not
start seeking precedents in the records of Trinity Col~
lege caddishmess. Mr. Bacon’s main position was that
the need for education as a practical requirement is not,
sufficiently appreciated in Ireland. Sufficiently familiar
with all forms of impractical education, we have come to:
look on education and impracticability as almost
synonymous.

“So far is the general body of our people from ap-
preciating the desperate nature of their situation in
this respect, that it is only comparatively few Irishmen
—only those who themselves have been more favoured
in the matter of education, and those who have come
in contact with and felt the pressure of competition with
those nations whose natural resources have been de-
veloped by modern science—it is only they, and by no
means all even of them, who seem to realise the dis-
advantages which the masses of the people have to en-
dure without understanding.

“The key to the whole anomaly of the situation, I
think, may be given in a sentence: ‘The democratic
University is unknown in this country, and being un-
known, its absence conveys no sense of loss to the great
bulk of our people.’ ”’ :

Education in Ireland has up to this been regarded
chiefly as a means of raising oneself from the ranks of
the plundered into those of the plunderers. Learning
was like Penelope, for he who wooed her gained the
right to a life of ease at the public expense.

It is far from surprising, however, that when educa-
tion of all kinds is denied them, people are little solici-
tous as to what kind they should receive. When, for

Soluip na nSaevedl.—All Irish Irishmen should insist on getting
tho new Irish Matoh, mado im Dublin by PATERSON & 0., and thus,
support the Industrial Revival.

‘nstance, the six hundred lay Catholic students attend-
ing lectures in Dublin receive an endowment of £6,000
2 year whilst a thousand lay Protestant studénts of
-equal ability veceive at the lowest computation £50,000
-a year, men’s minds are more likely to be occupied with
the demand for some cducational provision than for a
provision of a particular kind., Again men ever de-
sire much more the known than the unknown. What
incalculable good the present National Library does;
yet the want was but little felt, until it was established.
Again we can see with our own eyes the great results of
Intermediate education, and even of those few thousand
pounds spent on Royal University education. But be-
fore 1879, people in Treland probably never appreciated
the frightful grievance under which they laboured,
though when you come to think of it, it was an appalling
sone. It has often seemed to me, in truth, that if one is
seeking for examples of Irish oppression, he will almost
find more real grievance in the half-century preceding
1880 than in the days of the Penal Laws. And yet men
knew so little of better things that they scarce realised
the full extent of the oppression under which they
laboured. So, too, it will require an Ireland flourish-
ing as the result of proper and properly endowed edu-
cation to understand how great is our misgovernment
in this respect to-day.

In justice to our masters, we should recognise with
Mr. Bacon that England only treated us as she treated
herself. Having, unlike Germany and America, failed
to appreciate the value of real education, she naturally
did not waste money on such doctrinaire ideas in Ire-
land, but confined her ameliorative efforts to the ample
provision of judges and policemen, and the development
of our resources by Royal Commissions—in fact, to
practical methods generally. Meanwhile France, Ger-
many, and America went on educating themselves and
narrowing England’s industrial lead. Ireland stayed
where she was ; she was too lazy to succeed. She lacked
the distinctive virtues of England, and it never oc-
curred to anyone that she might acquire these of any
other country. To-day England is beginning to go in
for education herself, and the Treasury is engaged in
saving on the salaries of Irish school-teachers.

No doubt the recent provision for Technical educa-
tion in Ireland is, if efficiently worked, a step in the
right direction, but as the provision for the higher
-education of three-fourths of the population continues

rigidly fixed at £6,000 a year, we can easily under-
stand the effect of setting up an institution without a
.roof. Unless proper provision for University education
is made and the rule of the endowment does not con-
tinue to be—Catholics, £10 a head ; Protestants, £50 a
head—something like the law about horses in the Penal
times—we may well agree with Mr. Bacon, that,
as compared with the systems of instruction working in
foreign countries, our system will yield but meagre re-

sults in comparison.
Mr. Bacon made an excellent point in regard to the
ladder of Technical education.

““The great majority of our poorer classes become,
-or attempt to become, wage earners about the age of
fifteen. A certain amount of help towards realizing
‘this object is now supplied by the Technical course pro-
vided in the schools. But once they have passed this
‘age any further progress, for most of them, depends on
‘the genius and on the unaided efforts of the individual.
“This is the stage at which the provident administrations
of countries like the United States step in to help the
more ambitious of their young workers. The youth
who has learned the rudiments of a trade at the Tech-
mnical School is encouraged, if his work is of a pro-
mising nature, to increase his knowledge at the higher
‘craft school, and later, to make himself thoroughl
‘master of his calling by a course at the Technical Col-
lege. e may, at this point, be classed amongst those
who have been called ‘the non-commissioned officers of
industry,” and he is fitted in his turn to take a part in
the practical training of his young fellow-countrymen.
Nor even, then, has he exhausted all the facilities
afforded him. For there are still open to him the
Technical University and the University proper, in

THE LEADER:

DECEMBER -2. 1905.

which he may reach the final stage of his eyolution from
the humble citizen’s son into a leader of the nation’s
industries. Until quite recently the condition of Irish
industrial enterprise was not such as to render the
establishment of higher Technical Schools and Colleges
a pressing necessity. But the impetus which native
industries have received during the last few years,
especially since the advent of the LzADER, has given a
new and urgent aspect to the question which the in-
terests of the country cannot suffer to be neglected.’”
Another subject fo which Mr. Bacon very properly
drew attention was the value of non-technical or
‘“liberal ’ education in the training for industry, a
lesson learnt long since by Germany. No doubt Ml
Healy was right when he said to Mr. Courtney, “Did
you ever know a senior wrangler who was not a fool ?”’
but as he himself bore witness, it is not the talented and
idiotic exception, but the rise in the general average
that counts. No one would like to walk like a drill-
sergeant, and it is often the man who gets first in
his final that does worst at his profession, but liberal
and theoretical education, as it were, improve the breed
and increase general efficiency, when perhaps their most
shining products are often of as little use for practical
purposes as a racehorse. Mr. Bacon, as I said, recom-
mended general education ; he considered that even the
study of useless superfluous matters, such as national
language and literature, might in some mysterious way
fit a man for the industrial battle.  Of course, as he
told us, economists have long since pointed out the
futility of such matters. Moreover, we have seen how
efficient education has been in Ireland for fifty years
when the children’s time was not wasted in such studies.
Still Mr. Bacon would have us give up spending our
money usefully. He wants to do silly things like other
nations, and instead of developing our resources, go in
for developing ourselves. In which policy I, humbly,
venture to concur. CHANEL.

EADERS of the LEapER have no doubt some re-

inspection, from hearsay in others. But whichever it
be, it is scarcely necessary to re-tell what a great suc-

proper and the clearest way of assisting in forming an
opinion on the present exhibition to compare this with
last year’s display, and so try to gauge the progress
made. And it must at once be conceded that this year’s
is a marked improvement in many ways. First, it is far
more attractive both as regards the allotment of the ex-

finish. The Association’s experience has enabled it to
dispose of the exhibits in a more artistic and tasteful ar-

effectiveness in display and greater variety for the
visitor. The manufacturers also, on their part, have
made good progress in the arrangement of their stalls
and in the style and finish of their products. In fact,
all round there seems to be less of the amateur” in

containing 63 exhibitors, of whom some also exhibit
their agencies, but one would like to see more working
exhibits whore possible, only two this year, Johnson’s
umbrellas and Ryan’s soap being so represented. The

noy and Douglas tweeds, were unrepresented, we had
soveral Dark brothers and new industries coming into

BALLYMOTE.

NE would think that at this stage of the Irish Re-
vival movement every town at least in far back
Connaught would be bestirring itself in the interests of
that movement. Yet, there are several towns in Con-
naught where the inhabitants—Irish, of course, “O!
God Save Ireland,” ““ The devil take the grabbers!”
otc.—don’t know the colour of O’Growney Book I.,
and plod their way in ignorance of any boots, save
——’s from Glasgow, and who would scorn the idea of
wearing anything not stamped with the hall-mark of
““Shoddy ”” from England. There are Gaelic classes, if
you please, in the heart of London; the “smart’” towns
among the bogs of Connaught have but a very faint
idea of what the Gaelic League is; what its tenets are,
if it has any tenets at all; and apparently don’t care a
jack straw if the Gaelic League were swept clean out of
Ireland.

Personally, I have had experience of three or four of
these towns; in my opinion they are as bad as their
most vindictive enemy could with decency wish them.
The subject of the present article is about the worst.

Ballymote is a railway town in the county Sligo. It
is built on the side of a hill. It boasts of six ‘‘streets,”’
accommodates a population of twelve hundred odd.
Some few years ago the streets of Ballymote were
named after six of our venerable deceased martyrs and
statesmen. The honoured name of Dan O’Connell was
selected ; so was that of Emmet, of Grattan, and of
Tone. And a small block of houses gloried in the novel
designation of ‘ Teeling’’ Street. It was a brave
spurt; but I greatly fear that the old names—sugges-
tive appellations—‘‘ Church Street,”” ‘‘ Jail Street,”
““Mill Street,”” ‘‘ Newtown ’’—stuck in the minds of
the lethargic populace. It was a good thought which
prompted those at the head of the ‘ christening *’ de-
monstration to nail green name-boards to the corner
houses. The honoured names are on the walls anyhow,
but, to my knowledge, the prudent Burghers, will not
risk disgracing their British notepaper or their British
tongues by using those honoured names.

In the first place, let us consider how Ballymote has
treated the Language question. Has it a branch of the
Gaelic League? Has it any facilities for establishing
a branch? It has the facilities, but has not availed of
them. There are about half-a-dozen as staunch Gaelic
Leaguers, as may anywhere be found, in Ballymote,
but all efforts on their part towards the organisation of
a local branch have been attended by utter failure. If
the people of Ballymote had the earnest desire to foster
their native language they could not complain of a
lack of teachers. But unfortunately that spirit of
shoneenism, of Anglicisation, has so completely sapped
their better feelings, that those people’ give' not one
thought to the work. Tlere is: a little instance: of the
S '’ ofi Ballymote. Two years:ago a.towns-
man, a thorough Gaelic scholar, attempted! to start: a
language class. He was a. courageous, conscientious
man, otherwise he. would not have stuck to his class:so
dotorminedly as he did, for a whole season, with it
plain before his very eyes from the start that his
endeavours were only to result in pitiable failure. ‘He
‘was prepared to incur every expense, as to lighting and

But of what avail is it that Irish be taught in the

boys and girls may continue the study of the Language,

case of wasting time, pouring water into a sieve, learn-
ing the rudiments of a Language only to forget it again.

Ballymote has done nothing for the Language,
neither has it a notion of doing anything for it, and
still it has the impudence to consider itself a patriotic
Irish town.

There are twenty-five licensed drinking houses in

TRADE NOTES.

HE Amalgamated Society of Tailors is a body, we
understand, which is established very properly

to protect the rights of working tailors,  What are
known in the trade as the “log prices,”” or, in other
words, what are considered fair wages for working
tailors under trade union conditions are, we are in-
formed, regulated by mutual agreement between the re-
presentatives of this society and the houses in the tai-
loring business; and we also understand that these

THE LEADER.

DECEMBER 2. 1905.
prices are supposed to be uniform throughout all tailor-
ing establishments employing union labour. We have
heard a statement that a certain tailoring firm in Dub-
lin has had a special log struck for it which gives it an
advantage over the other Dublin firms; and we should
like to know if this statement is correct. If it is cor-
rect, we are sure the Dublin branch of the society will
be glad to offer an explanation of this discrimination.
Our readers will have secen the advertisement of
Messrs T. W. Drabble and Co., Sheffield, that has been
appearing in the LeADER for some time past. Messrs.
Drabble and Co. advertise cutlery of various kinds,
and as their name and address have figured prominently
in every announcement, there has been no attempt to
disguise or obscure the manufacturing source of the
goods they offer for sale. Their advertisement which
appears in this issue contains the words—"If you can-
not buy cutlery of Irish manufacture, buy from the
makers;’’ and the “ copy ’’ inserted in previous issues
relating to razors had the equally frank announcement—
“ Sheffield to the front.” We give these quo-
tations merely to emphasise the fact that Messrs.
Drabble and Co. make no offer in our pages to
masquerade as Irish manufacture the articles which
they have put on the Irish market on their merits.
When this English firm offered their advertisement,
we decided not to accept it if we found any Irish
concern manufacturing in the same lines. We made
inquiries of an Irish firm in the cutlery business whose
advertisement appeared in contemporaries in sympathy
with the Irish Ireland movement, and who, we were
under the impression, were manufacturers in that way
themselves. A representative of this firm promptly in-
formed us, however, that although they executed re-
pairs they did not manufacture, and that as far as they
were aware the goods advertised by Messrs. Drabble and
Co. would not conflict with any other Irish industry.
A member of the latter firm when in Dublin shortly
afterwards called on us, and frankly pointed out him-
self the articles that Ireland, as far as he knew, was
able to manufacture in cutlery. For we are glad to
say that Ireland does make something in this line, and
we should like to see that something developed as far as
possible. Messrs Broderick and Sons, of Capel street,
Dublin, for instance, in addition to supplying and re-
pairing all sorts of cutlery, are manufacturers of but-
chers’ knives and tools.  Messrs. Broderick and Sons,
we are informed, forge these knives out of the bar steel,
and make and fit the handles, etc. They also make to
order other special kinds of knives and tools. . But-
chers’ aprons are an indispensable complement to but-
chers’ cutlery, and Messrs. Broderick and Sons, Dublin.
supply Irish butchers with Dublin-made aprons as well
as Dublin-made knives. Mr. F. Byrne, 28 Francis St.,
is another Dublin knife and edged tool maker. He
makes various kinds of butchers’ knives, poulterers’ and
bakers’ knives, book-binders’, shoe-makers’ knives, etc.
But so far as general cutlery is concerned, we have not
heard of any Irish manufacturers; and while razors,
scissors, table-knives, pocket-knives, forks, etc., are

np(c made in Ireland, we presume we must
cither go  without them or depend upon
importation.  That being so, we had no hesi-

tation in accepting the Sheffield firm’s advertise-
ment for these things, as we would similarly have had
no hesitation in accepting an advertisement for Indian
or Chinese teas. In addition to their razors, Messrs.
Drabble, as above stated, have put scissors, pocket-
knives, table-knives, forks, etc., on the Irish market.
